target.title: fix #94166: memory-core OpenAI-compatible embeddings: honor provider request.allowPrivateNetwork
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `memory-core` OpenAI-compatible embedding requests block a configured private/internal endpoint even when the operator explicitly trusts that provider, because the embedding SSRF policy only allowlists the exact hostname and never inherits the provider's transport trust. (Fixes #94166.)
- Solution: When the backing provider opts in via `models.providers.<id>.request.allowPrivateNetwork`, honor that same transport trust for the memory embedding fetch — mirroring the model-provider self-hosted path.
- What changed: `ConfiguredEmbeddingProvider` now reads `request.allowPrivateNetwork`; a new `buildEmbeddingSsrfPolicy` adds `allowPrivateNetwork: true` to the embedding SSRF policy only when that opt-in is present.
- What did NOT change: the SSRF-safe default. With no opt-in, private/internal IPs stay blocked and only the exact configured hostname is allowlisted; metadata/link-local protections are untouched.
- Why it matters / User impact: self-hosted / private-network embedding endpoints become usable via existing provider config, without a localhost proxy workaround and without relaxing SSRF defaults for anyone else.

## Competition / linked PR analysis

- **Linked PR(s):** #94180 (`feat(memory-core): allow private network endpoints for memory embeddings`). (The public-contract scan also flagged #92307, which is unrelated — it is about exec-approval startup warnings, not embeddings.)
- **Gap in linked PR(s):** #94180 introduces a **new memory-specific config knob** `memorySearch.remote.network.dangerouslyAllowPrivateNetwork`. That adds a second, parallel trust surface for a decision the provider already expresses via `models.providers.<id>.request.allowPrivateNetwork`, and operators must learn/maintain a memory-only flag distinct from the provider transport trust they already configured.
- **Why this patch is better/canonical:** It reuses the **existing** provider-level `request.allowPrivateNetwork` transport-trust contract (the same one the model-provider self-hosted path honors), so memory embeddings inherit the operator's already-declared provider trust instead of inventing a new surface. This is the narrowest existing-surface fix ClawSweeper identified as preferred, with no new config to document or migrate.
- **Local real behavior proof advantage:** Unit tests assert the policy propagation both ways (opt-in ⇒ `allowPrivateNetwork: true`, no opt-in ⇒ default blocking preserved with the hostname still pinned), so the SSRF-safe default is regression-locked.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** A configured provider with `request.allowPrivateNetwork: true` makes the memory embedding SSRF policy allow that private endpoint (hostname still pinned); without the opt-in the policy keeps blocking private/internal IPs.
- **Real environment tested:** Linux worktree on latest `origin/main`, Node 22.22.0, pnpm 11.2.2; ran the embedding-provider vitest suite (the code path that builds `client.ssrfPolicy` consumed by the guarded fetch).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/openai-compatible-embedding-provider.test.ts`.
- **Evidence after fix:**

```text
RUN  v4.1.8
 ✓ unit-fast  src/plugins/openai-compatible-embedding-provider.test.ts (26 tests)
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  26 passed (26)
```

- **Observed result after fix:** New test "honors a configured provider request.allowPrivateNetwork for the embedding SSRF policy" ⇒ `ssrfPolicy.allowPrivateNetwork === true` with `allowedHostnames === ["llm.internal"]`; new test "keeps the SSRF-safe default …" ⇒ `allowPrivateNetwork === undefined`.
- **What was not tested:** No live private-network embedding endpoint was stood up (no such environment available), so end-to-end delivery against a real private `/v1/embeddings` was not exercised; the change is unit-verified at the policy-construction layer that the guarded fetch consumes. **Product/security decision disclosure:** this PR adopts the *provider-inheritance* design; maintainers should confirm that memory embeddings are intended to inherit `models.providers.<id>.request.allowPrivateNetwork` rather than gaining a memory-specific knob (the alternative #94180 chose).
- **Fix classification:** Root cause fix

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target test file:** `src/plugins/openai-compatible-embedding-provider.test.ts`
- **Scenario locked in:** opt-in provider ⇒ embedding policy allows private network (hostname still pinned); no opt-in ⇒ no `allowPrivateNetwork` (default block preserved).
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** it pins the exact policy-construction decision at the owner module without needing a live private endpoint, and regression-locks the SSRF-safe default.

## Merge risk

- **Risk labels considered:** security-boundary, auth-provider, compatibility.
- **Risk explanation:** This touches the SSRF trust boundary for memory embeddings. A wrong relaxation could broaden private-network access.
- **Why acceptable:** Access is gated strictly behind an explicit per-provider opt-in (`request.allowPrivateNetwork`), the hostname allowlist is still pinned, the default (no opt-in) path is unchanged and regression-tested, and the opt-in mirrors the existing model-provider self-hosted trust contract rather than inventing a broader one.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** The source of truth for self-hosted transport trust is the provider's `request.allowPrivateNetwork` contract, but the memory embedding adapter built its SSRF policy purely from the base URL hostname and never inherited that provider state, so an explicitly-trusted private endpoint was still blocked.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** It threads the existing provider transport-trust contract into the embedding policy at the owner module (the embedding client builder), instead of adding a downstream override or a separate memory-only knob.
- **Fix classification:** Root cause fix
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High
- **Patch quality notes:** No skipped tests / suppressions; the default-blocking path is explicitly preserved and tested.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** `models.providers.<id>.request.allowPrivateNetwork` is the owner contract for provider transport trust; this change makes the memory embedding SSRF policy consume that contract, with no change to default SSRF blocking or to how endpoints are otherwise resolved.
target.author: mushuiyu886
